items.q: Why do designers often use 5th to 95th percentile data instead of just the 50th?
items.correct: To accommodate the majority of users rather than just the average.
items.misconception: Because the 50th percentile is too unreliable.
items.why: Students think designing for the average user is best, but it excludes most people. The 5th-95th range covers 90% of users - designing only for the mean fails everyone above or below it.

items.q: What is the purpose of a jig in manufacturing?
items.correct: To guide a tool and ensure consistent, accurate repeated operations.
items.misconception: To hold the workpiece very tightly so it does not move.
items.why: Students confuse jigs with fixtures. A fixture HOLDS the workpiece; a jig GUIDES the tool (e.g., a drill jig guides the drill bit to the correct position) - though the terms are sometimes used loosely.

items.correct: A small integrated chip containing CPU, memory, and I/O for controlling a device.
items.misconception: A very small computer like a laptop.
items.random_wrong: A remote control for a TV.
items.why: Students think microcontrollers are mini-PCs. They are specialised: limited resources, designed for a single embedded task. No operating system needed - just dedicated code running directly.
The answer examiners want is: Oscillating. “Rotary” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A cam turns rotary motion into.
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Reciprocating. “Linear continuous” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many types of motion are listed?
items.why: The real figure is 4. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
